---
title: "A minimal-revision roadmap (and the questions to expect)"
---

The dissertation is done, and the committee approved it. What follows is not a verdict on
that work — it is a runway for what comes next. The central arguments about MFRM and HRM
*sensitivity* and *robustness* are worth publishing; the only question is how to present
them so a peer reviewer — who has not met the author and will read quickly — cannot find a
foothold for doubt. This chapter pulls the seven critique chapters into one sequenced plan,
then gathers the questions a reviewer is most likely to ask.

## The minimum that lets the central conclusions stand

The headline claims do not require new data. They require tighter grounding in what the
reported numbers actually measure. None of what follows requires collecting a single new
rating or rebuilding a study from scratch — every fix is a targeted addition or a reframing:
better diagnostics where the estimation went unchecked, cleaner operationalizations where
the manipulations drifted from their definitions, and modest housekeeping where the
reporting left questions open. The architecture of both simulation studies (the
dissertation's Chapters 3 and 4) is sound; tighten the scaffolding and the same intellectual
argument becomes airtight.

## A sequenced plan

*("Ch." below refers to the chapters of **this companion**, not the dissertation; the order
follows logical dependency rather than chapter number.)*

Work in this order, because each tier clears the ground for the next. **Tier 1 comes first**
because if "sensitivity" is measuring the wrong thing (a) or the HRM estimates are unstable
(b), none of the other numbers can be interpreted at all. **Tier 2** then fixes what the
manipulations actually estimate, and **Tier 3** is genuine housekeeping — real, but not
blocking.

### Tier 1 — Reframe and re-estimate (highest leverage)

1. **(a) Re-operationalize "sensitivity" as detection.** For every condition, report TPR,
   FPR, and ROC-AUC at the cut score (and parameter recovery), with **both** normal and
   effect raters in the analysis so the false-positive rate is visible; retire or explicitly
   justify the η² ≥ .01 bar. This single move reframes both studies and answers the most
   fundamental objection (Ch. 5). *Effort: medium — one analysis pass, no new simulation.*
2. **(b) Re-estimate the HRM with adequate MCMC.** Run ≥ 4 independent chains with a longer
   warm-up; report R-hat, bulk/tail ESS, and MCSE for φ and ψ in every condition; do a
   prior-sensitivity check with a log-scale prior for ψ; and flag the misfit ψ as
   *non-estimable* rather than reporting a sampler-generated value (Ch. 1 + Ch. 4).
   *Effort: higher — re-running estimation is the bottleneck; the model itself does not change.*
3. **(c) Diagnose the severe-location collapse.** Show parameter recovery (bias, RMSE,
   coverage) for the severe-rater location against its generating value condition by
   condition; audit the centering constraint; and disentangle the sample-size confound
   (N, rater count, and effect-rater count move together). If a one-line centering fix
   corrects it, document that transparently (Ch. 2). *Effort: low–medium.*

### Tier 2 — Re-operationalize the manipulations

4. **(d) Replace α = 0 "misfit" with a realistic range.** A zero slope is a uniform random
   rater, not a noisy human; use a slope gradient (e.g., α ∈ {0.8, 0.5, 0.2}) plus a halo or
   central-tendency mechanism, and add a **neutral cross-generative** cell so neither model
   is graded on home turf (Ch. 3). *Effort: higher — new simulation cells, same framework.*
5. **(e) Re-manipulate local dependence on the residual scale.** Compute the realized
   *residual* correlation per condition; the marginal LI baseline depends on the unit (≈ 0.79
   per total score, ≈ 0.43 per item), so 0.2 injects *negative* residual dependence while 0.6
   is a genuine positive violation under the "among items" reading (0.9 on either) — re-manipulate
   on the residual scale (a shared rater-pair random effect or copula) and re-scope the "MFRM is
   robust" claim to the conditions that are genuine violations (Ch. 6). *Effort: medium.*

### Tier 3 — Housekeeping

6. **(f) Reconcile the condition count and release the log.** Resolve whether the grid is
   3⁴ = 81 or 4 × 3³ = 108, trace the stray n = 900, and make the condition log /
   simulation code available as supplementary material (Ch. 7). *Effort: low.*
7. **(g) Analyze at the replicate level.** Replace per-indicator ANOVAs that ignore
   clustering with replicate-level summaries or a mixed-effects model, and either justify
   η² ≥ .01 against a detection baseline or replace it (Ch. 7). *Effort: low–medium.*
8. **(h) Double-code ~20% of the systematic-review articles.** Have a second coder apply the scheme
   independently and report Cohen's κ (or Krippendorff's α) — additive, but expected
   (Ch. 7). *Effort: low.*

## The questions to expect, gathered

| Chapter | The sharpest question a reviewer will ask |
|---|---|
| **1** — ψ identifiability | "In what sense is a reported ψ of 16–32 an estimate, given the profile likelihood is flat for ψ ≳ 5 — and why average a non-estimable quantity into an ANOVA?" |
| **2** — Location collapse | "Why does the severe-rater location fall monotonically with N when more data should stabilize it? Show recovery vs. true values, condition by condition." |
| **3** — Misfit operationalization | "Is a slope of 0 (uniform noise) a valid model of inaccuracy, and how do you separate the MFRM's generate-equals-fit advantage from a real finding?" |
| **4** — MCMC adequacy | "What are the ESS and MCSE of φ and ψ at 700 draws from one chain — are the interpreted differences actually larger than the MCSE? How was R-hat computed from one chain?" |
| **5** — Sensitivity definition | "Your definition is detection — why report η² of means on effect raters only, not TPR/FPR/ROC-AUC at your cut? And why is η² ≥ .01 'practical' when it is near-chance?" |
| **6** — Dependence scale | "The marginal LI baseline depends on the unit (≈ 0.79 per total score, ≈ 0.43 per item); under the 'among items' reading, 0.2 is below it (not a violation) while 0.6 and 0.9 are genuine violations. Which scale were the targets on — and can a null be read as robustness? Report the residual correlation per condition." |
| **7** — Housekeeping | "Are there 81 or 108 conditions — can you supply the full grid? And what is the inter-coder reliability for the systematic-review coding?" |

## Why this is worth doing

Each item on this list is a methodological strength waiting to be unlocked. Reviewers do
not reward authors who anticipated no criticism; they reward authors who have already
thought the critique through and answered it in the manuscript. Reporting ROC-AUC
beside η² is not a concession of weakness — it demonstrates command of the
detection-theoretic foundations of the construct. Running four chains and reporting
R-hat is not an apology for the dissertation — it demonstrates what adequate
MCMC looks like. Documenting the centering audit turns a potential confound into a
finding in its own right. The revised studies stop being merely *plausible* — where a reader
must trust the setup — and become *persuasive*, where a skeptical reader can check the work
and find it standing.

The hardest part is already done: two original simulation studies were designed and run, a
systematic review completed, and a full dissertation written. The revisions ahead
are, by comparison, targeted and finite — and every simulation study in the top measurement
journals went through exactly this kind of sharpening. The architecture of the work is sound,
and the central conclusions are reachable from it.

::: {.callout-tip}
## The eight-item checklist
- [ ] **(a)** Ch. 5 — Report TPR / FPR / ROC-AUC (+ recovery); include normal *and* effect raters; retire or justify η² ≥ .01
- [ ] **(b)** Ch. 1 + 4 — Re-run HRM: ≥ 4 chains, longer warm-up, report R-hat / ESS / MCSE, prior sensitivity on ψ, flag ψ non-estimable for noise raters
- [ ] **(c)** Ch. 2 — Recovery (bias / RMSE / coverage) for severe-rater location per condition; centering audit; disentangle the N–rater-count confound
- [ ] **(d)** Ch. 3 — Replace α = 0 with a slope gradient + realistic mechanisms; add neutral cross-generative cells
- [ ] **(e)** Ch. 6 — Report realized residual correlation per condition; re-manipulate on the residual scale; re-scope the "robust" claim
- [ ] **(f)** Ch. 7 — Reconcile 81 vs. 108 (+ n = 900); release the condition log
- [ ] **(g)** Ch. 7 — Replicate-level / mixed-effects analysis; justify or replace η² ≥ .01
- [ ] **(h)** Ch. 7 — Double-code ~20% of the systematic-review articles; report Cohen's κ
:::
